d: Mobile small: 320-374dp. Covers iPhone SE, older small-screen devices, and accessibility large-text modes on modern phones. Minimum viable target for most apps. At 320dp wide, space is extremely tight — use single-column layouts, full-width buttons, and compact navigation. Always test at 320dp to catch text overflow, truncation, and touch-target crowding issues.

Mobile standard: 375-413dp. Covers iPhone 14/15/16 (393dp), Pixel phones (~393dp), Galaxy S series (~360dp). This is the primary mobile design target — most mobile users fall in this range. Design key mobile screens at 375dp or 393dp. Single-column layout with 16dp margins and 8-16dp gutters. This breakpoint is the starting point for mobile-first design.

Mobile large: 414-479dp. Covers Plus and Max models (iPhone 16 Plus: 430dp, iPhone 16 Pro Max: 440dp), large Android devices. Extra width allows slightly more breathing room but still single-column layout. Cards can show more horizontal content. Do not add a second column at this size — the extra space should improve spacing and readability, not cram in more content.

Tablet portrait: 600-839dp. Covers iPad mini (744dp), standard iPad (810dp), Android tablets. Transition point from single-column to multi-column layouts. Use 8-column grid with 24dp margins and 16-24dp gutters. Sidebars may appear as overlays or slide-out panels. Cards can display in 2-column grids. Navigation can shift from bottom tabs to sidebar or top nav. Material 3 classifies 600dp as start of medium window class.

meta.devices: iPad mini, standard iPad, Android tablets

d: Tablet landscape: 840-1023dp. Covers iPad landscape (1080dp logical), iPad Pro 11-inch, Android tablets in landscape. Full multi-column layouts work here — sidebars with main content area, 2-3 column card grids. This range bridges tablet and desktop patterns. Material 3 classifies 840dp as start of expanded window class. Navigation should use persistent sidebar or top nav. Content width can be constrained with max-width for readability.

Desktop small: 1024-1279dp. Covers laptops, small external monitors, and browser windows that are not maximized. Full desktop layout with sidebar navigation, multi-column content, and standard desktop interaction patterns (hover states, tooltips). Use 12-column grid with 24dp margins and 24dp gutters. Content should have a max-width to prevent extreme stretching. This is the minimum desktop target for most web applications.

Desktop standard: 1280-1439dp (common monitors, popular Figma artboard size). Desktop large: 1440-1919dp (large monitors, default web design target). Desktop XL: 1920dp+ (wide/ultra-wide screens). At 1440dp+ use max content width of 1200-1440dp to prevent content from stretching across the entire viewport. Center content with auto margins. Side panels and secondary content areas can use the extra space. Design primary desktop layouts at 1440dp.

RULE: Design mobile-first, progressively enhancing for larger screens. Progression order: 320 (stress test) -> 375 (primary mobile) -> 414 (large mobile) -> 768 (tablet) -> 1024 (desktop small) -> 1440 (desktop standard). Mobile-first forces content prioritization: decide what matters most when space is scarce. Each breakpoint adds layout complexity — never remove essential content at smaller sizes, only restructure how it is presented.

Material 3 window size classes: compact (width < 600dp), medium (600-839dp), expanded (840dp+). These map to phone, tablet portrait, and tablet landscape/desktop. Apple size classes: compact width (< 414pt), regular width (>= 414pt); compact height (< 414pt), regular height (>= 414pt). Apple uses size class pairs (horizontal, vertical) to determine layout. Design components to respond to these platform-defined classes rather than arbitrary pixel breakpoints when building native apps.

Constraints control how a child element behaves when its PARENT frame is resized manually. Horizontal options: Left (pin to left edge), Right (pin to right edge), Left & Right (stretch with parent), Center (stay centered), Scale (resize proportionally with parent). Vertical options: Top (pin to top), Bottom (pin to bottom), Top & Bottom (stretch vertically), Center (stay centered vertically), Scale. Constraints appear in the right panel when a layer inside a non-auto-layout frame is selected.

Left & Right constraint: element stretches horizontally as parent width changes, maintaining distance from both edges. Use for full-width headers, content areas, and dividers. Center: element maintains its size and stays centered regardless of parent size. Use for centered logos, modals, and hero content. Scale: element resizes proportionally with parent — both size and position scale. Use for illustrations, decorative graphics, and complex visual compositions that should maintain proportions. Combine H and V independently: e.g., Left & Right + Top = stretch width, pin to top.

RULE: Constraints are DISABLED inside auto layout frames. Auto layout uses its own sizing model (hug, fill, fixed) instead of constraints. Use constraints only for: non-auto-layout frames, decorative/overlapping elements positioned absolutely, background images pinned to frame edges, and elements that need to scale proportionally with their container. If you need responsive behavior inside an auto layout frame, use fill-container sizing and min/max width instead of constraints. Constraints and auto layout are mutually exclusive positioning systems.

Pattern: Fluid width. Set parent frame to fill-container width (or a fixed artboard width). Set children to fill-container so they expand to use available space. Text elements set to fill-width will reflow as the container changes size. This creates naturally responsive layouts without breakpoint logic. Combine with min-width and max-width on children to prevent them from becoming too narrow or too wide. Fluid width is the foundation of responsive auto layout design in Figma.

Pattern: Wrap layout. Set a horizontal auto layout frame to wrap mode. Children flow left to right and automatically move to the next line when they exceed the parent width. Ideal for tag lists, card grids, button groups, and gallery layouts. Set children to a fixed or min-width so they wrap predictably. Combine with fill-container on children and gap settings to create flexible grid-like layouts. Wrap mode was added in 2024 and eliminates the need for manual row/column management in many responsive scenarios.

Pattern: Sidebar + main content. Create a horizontal auto layout frame. Add sidebar child with fixed width (240-320dp). Add main content child set to fill-container. The main content area expands/contracts while the sidebar remains constant width. For collapsible sidebar: use a boolean variable or component property to toggle sidebar visibility, or swap between variants with and without sidebar. On mobile, the sidebar typically becomes a drawer overlay or bottom sheet.

Pattern: Stacking/reflow. For layouts that are horizontal on desktop and vertical on mobile: create two variants of the component — one with horizontal auto layout, one with vertical. Swap variants per breakpoint frame. Alternatively, use wrap mode on a horizontal auto layout: on wide screens items sit side by side, on narrow screens they wrap and stack vertically. Wrap mode is preferred when the reflow is simple. Use explicit variants when the layout structure fundamentally changes (different spacing, alignment, element order).

Pattern: Flexible card grid. Create a horizontal auto layout with wrap enabled. Add card children with min-width (e.g., 280dp) and fill-container width. Cards expand to fill available space and wrap to the next row when there is not enough room. Set gap for consistent spacing between cards. This creates a grid that automatically adjusts column count based on available width: 1 column on mobile, 2 on tablet, 3-4 on desktop. No manual column management needed.

Four approaches to responsive components: (1) Variant-based: create explicit Mobile/Tablet/Desktop variants, swap manually per breakpoint artboard. Most control, most maintenance. (2) Flexible auto layout: single component with hug/fill sizing that naturally adapts to any width. Fewest variants, most maintainable. (3) Responsive variables (2025): use variable collection with device modes (Mobile/Tablet/Desktop) to swap spacing, sizing, and visibility values. (4) Wrap mode: horizontal auto layout that stacks when narrow, spreads when wide. RULE: Prefer approach 2 (flexible auto layout) as the default. Use approach 1 only when layout fundamentally changes between breakpoints.

Responsive variables (2025 feature): Create a variable collection with modes named Mobile, Tablet, Desktop. Define spacing variables: content-padding (16/24/32dp per mode), section-gap (24/40/64dp), grid-columns (4/8/12), card-min-width (280/320/360dp). Define sizing variables: sidebar-width (0/280/320dp), max-content-width (375/768/1200dp). Apply the appropriate mode to each top-level artboard frame — all children using these variables automatically inherit the correct responsive values. This eliminates manual per-breakpoint overrides for spacing and sizing.

RULE: Use responsive variables for spacing and sizing that changes per breakpoint — padding, gaps, widths, and margins. Do NOT use responsive variables for layout direction or structural changes (use variant swaps for that). Name variables descriptively: 'spacing/content-padding', 'sizing/sidebar-width', 'grid/column-count'. Keep mode count small (3-4 modes: Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, optionally Desktop-XL). Apply modes at the top-level frame so they cascade to all nested components. Responsive variables work alongside auto layout — they control the values, auto layout controls the behavior.

Pixel density scales: @1x (mdpi, 160dpi) is the baseline used in Figma — 1pt equals 1px. @1.5x (hdpi, 240dpi) is Android-only. @2x (xhdpi, 320dpi, Retina) covers iPhone standard, iPad, and most Android flagships. @3x (xxhdpi, 480dpi, Super Retina) covers iPhone Pro/Plus/Max and high-end Android. @4x (xxxhdpi, 640dpi) covers some Android devices. SVG is resolution-independent vector format, preferred for icons and simple illustrations. RULE: Design at @1x in Figma. Export at all needed densities for the target platform.

t: Export rules for web and native
d: For web: export raster images at @1x and @2x minimum, use srcset attribute for responsive image selection. SVG preferred for icons and logos. For iOS: export @1x, @2x, @3x — Xcode asset catalogs expect these suffixes. For Android: export mdpi(@1x), hdpi(@1.5x), xhdpi(@2x), xxhdpi(@3x), xxxhdpi(@4x) into density-specific drawable folders. RULE: Design icons on a pixel-perfect grid at 24x24dp @1x for crisp rendering at all densities. Avoid odd-numbered dimensions for icons exported at @1.5x to prevent half-pixel blurring.

Minimum body text: 14sp/pt on mobile, 16sp/pt on desktop. NEVER go below 14sp/pt on any device. Type scale ratio: 1.2 (minor third) for mobile creates tighter hierarchy in limited space; 1.25 (major third) for desktop gives more dramatic hierarchy. Base size: 16sp/pt. Scale headings up per breakpoint but keep body and caption text consistent. Optimal line length: 45-75 characters per line — use max-width on text containers to constrain. RULE: Touch device inputs require minimum 16sp/pt text to prevent iOS auto-zoom on focus.

Mobile (320-599dp): 4-column grid, 16dp margins, 8-16dp gutters. Tablet (600-1023dp): 8-column grid, 24dp margins, 16-24dp gutters. Desktop (1024dp+): 12-column grid, 24-64dp margins, 24dp gutters. Max content width: 1200-1440dp to prevent content stretching on ultra-wide monitors — center the grid with auto margins beyond max width. In Figma: apply column grid style to each artboard frame, create separate grid styles per breakpoint (e.g., 'Grid/Mobile-4col', 'Grid/Tablet-8col', 'Grid/Desktop-12col').

RULE: Use a consistent gutter size across breakpoints — 16-24dp works well for most designs. Gutters should match or align with your spacing scale (8dp base). RULE: Margins increase with screen size to provide more breathing room on larger screens. Mobile margins (16dp) are tight to maximize content area. Desktop margins (24-64dp) are generous for visual comfort. Grid gutter and margin should be multiples of your base spacing unit (typically 8dp). Column count changes per breakpoint but gutter and base unit remain consistent for visual harmony.

t: Adaptive vs responsive design in Figma
d: Responsive design: single layout that fluidly adapts to any width using auto layout with fill-container sizing, min/max widths, and wrap mode. One frame, infinite widths. Adaptive design: distinct fixed layouts per breakpoint, each optimized for that size — different structure, navigation, and content arrangement. In Figma: responsive = one frame with flexible auto layout that you can resize. Adaptive = multiple artboards at specific widths (375, 768, 1024, 1440). RULE: Use responsive for components (they adapt within any container). Use adaptive for full-page layouts (pages often need fundamentally different structures per breakpoint). Hybrid approach is best: responsive components placed within adaptive page layouts.

Create artboards at each target breakpoint: 320 (stress test), 375 (mobile), 768 (tablet), 1024 (desktop small), 1440 (desktop standard). Manually resize artboard width horizontally to test fluid behavior between breakpoints. Check for: text wrapping and truncation, image cropping and aspect ratios, element stacking order, touch target sizes (minimum 44x44pt iOS / 48x48dp Android), spacing consistency, and horizontal overflow. Use prototype mode to test scroll behavior per breakpoint. RULE: Always test at 320dp width to catch edge cases — if it works at 320, it works everywhere.

Pattern: Adaptive text. Set text layers to fill-width inside an auto layout frame so text reflows as the container resizes. Apply min-width (e.g., 200dp) to prevent text from becoming unreadably narrow on small screens. Apply max-width (e.g., 680dp) to prevent lines from exceeding optimal 45-75 character line length on wide screens. Center the text container within a fill-width parent for balanced layouts on desktop. This single pattern handles text readability across all breakpoints without any variant swaps.

t: Grid auto layout with fractional tracks
d: Pattern: Grid layout using auto layout grid mode (2025). Apply grid layout direction to an auto layout frame and define column tracks using fractional units (1fr, 2fr) for proportional column sizing. Items reflow into available columns based on the frame width. Combine with min column width to control when columns collapse. This provides CSS Grid-like behavior directly in Figma. For simpler needs, the wrap mode pattern with min-width children achieves similar results without explicit track definitions.

RULE: Prefer flexible auto layout components (approach 2) as the default — one component that works at any width reduces variant explosion and maintenance burden. RULE: Use variant-based responsive components (approach 1) only when the layout fundamentally changes: different element order, entirely different navigation patterns, or content that is shown/hidden per breakpoint. RULE: Never create separate Mobile/Desktop variants if the only difference is spacing — use responsive variables instead. RULE: Document which breakpoints a component supports in its description. RULE: Test every responsive component at all target breakpoints before publishing to the library.
